coredata.dc:title: Preparation of pH sensitive film based on starch/carbon nano dots incorporating anthocyanin for monitoring spoilage of pork
coredata.dc:creator: Koshy, Rekha Rose
Koshy, Jijo Thomas
Mary, Siji K.
Sadanandan, Sandeep
Jisha, S.
Pothan, Laly A.
coredata.dc:description: Biodegradable colourimetric films incorporating anthocyanins which can respond to food spoilage is a novel and green development in packaging field. However, when it comes to commercial applications, these biodegradable matrices lack sufficient mechanical strength and water resistivity. Hence nanomaterials are being incorporated into the matrix to improve its properties. The aim of this work was to develop intelligent starch based biopolymer film by incorporating carbon dot (CD) and anthocyanin extracted from clitoria ternatea flower (CTE). The starch/CD/CTE film (SED) was prepared by solution casting technique and characterised as novel, high sensitive, pH indicator film having the potential to monitor the freshness of packed pork. SEM, FTIR and XRD analysis showed homogenous distribution of CD and CTE in starch matrix. SED films exhibited highest mechanical, barrier, thermal and antioxidant properties due to synergistic effect of CD and CTE. Besides, SED films exhibited colour variations in different pH due to the presence of anthocyanin in CTE. Hence SED film was used as a low cost visual indicator for monitoring freshness of packed pork sample. The film showed visual colour changes from purple to green as storage time increased. This study shows that food grade ingredients can be used to prepare active smart films which can in turn be used to monitor the freshness of food product like pork.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anthocyanin
Starch
Carbon dot
Soy protein

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of starch based indicator films
pH-sensing ability of CTE solution and SE, SED1, SED2 films
Anthocyanin concentration and colour of CTE in pH buffers
Physical appearance and color of the films
Physical properties of starch and starch indicator films
Colour responses of SE, SED1, SED2 films to pH changes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Colour variations of CTE solution in different pH buffer, (B) Physical appearance of S, SE, SED1 and SED2 films. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
SEM images of A) S, B) SE, C) SED1 and SED2 films.
A) FTIR and B) XRD of starch and starch indicator films.
TGA curve of starch and starch indicator films.

A) Mechanical properties and B) Antioxidant activity of starch and starch indicator films. Values are determined as mean ± SD (n = 3). Lower case letters (a–d) indicate significant difference (p < 0.05).

A) Colour changes and B) colour parameter values of starch indicator films in different pH buffer solutions. Values are determined as mean ± SD (n = 3). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Indicator film response towards A) 0.8 M and B) 1.4 M ammonia solutions. Values are determined as mean ± SD (n = 3). Lower case letters (a–f) indicate significant difference (p < 0.05).

Colour change in the SE, SED1 and SED2 indicator films attached to packages of pork on getting spoiled. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Thickness, Moisture Content, Swelling Degree, Water Solubility of films.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Values are determined as mean ± SD (n = 3). Lower case letters (a-h) in the same column indicate significant difference (p < 0.05).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The ΔE values of SE, SED0.6 and SED0.9 films after being exposed to different buffer solutions for 10 min.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Values are determined as mean ± SD (n = 3). Lower case letters (a-h) in the same column indicate.

head.ce:title: Preparation of pH sensitive film based on starch/carbon nano dots incorporating anthocyanin for monitoring spoilage of pork

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, CMS College, M G University, Kottayam, 686001, Kerala, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biodegradable colourimetric films incorporating anthocyanins which can respond to food spoilage is a novel and green development in packaging field. However, when it comes to commercial applications, these biodegradable matrices lack sufficient mechanical strength and water resistivity. Hence nanomaterials are being incorporated into the matrix to improve its properties. The aim of this work was to develop intelligent starch based biopolymer film by incorporating carbon dot (CD) and anthocyanin extracted from clitoria ternatea flower (CTE). The starch/CD/CTE film (SED) was prepared by solution casting technique and characterised as novel, high sensitive, pH indicator film having the potential to monitor the freshness of packed pork. SEM, FTIR and XRD analysis showed homogenous distribution of CD and CTE in starch matrix. SED films exhibited highest mechanical, barrier, thermal and antioxidant properties due to synergistic effect of CD and CTE. Besides, SED films exhibited colour variations in different pH due to the presence of anthocyanin in CTE. Hence SED film was used as a low cost visual indicator for monitoring freshness of packed pork sample. The film showed visual colour changes from purple to green as storage time increased. This study shows that food grade ingredients can be used to prepare active smart films which can in turn be used to monitor the freshness of food product like pork.

ce:section.ce:para: Active and intelligent packaging is a novel development in the area of packaging wherein a packaging material benefits from synergistic effect of both active (antimicrobial and antioxidant) and intelligent materials (indicating about the product freshness, temperature, safety etc.) incorporated in the packaging material (Wu et al., 2019). The main advantage of these kind of packaging is that it extends the shelf life of packed food materials and also inform the consumers about the quality of food without the need of opening the package. Compared to petroleum derived packaging materials, intelligent packaging material derived from natural biopolymers like polysaccharides and protein etc. have always been researchers favourite as it has the potentiality to reduce environmental pollution. Starch is one such renewable polysaccharide of interest which is widely used as biopolymer matrix due to its abundance, low cost, renewability and biodegradability (J. Chen et al., 2019). Starch can be easily converted to thermoplastic starch under high temperature in presence of plasticizers (Xue Mei et al., 2020). The applicability of thermoplastic starch based packaging materials can be enhanced by incorporating novel materials into starch matrix by which it can respond to the changes in the internal and external factors and inform the consumers about the status of the packed food materials by direct vision without actually consuming it. In this context, pH sensitive dyes and extracts which can respond to pH changes by changing their colour are being widely used as a component of intelligent starch matrix. Anthocyanin is one such pH sensitive natural pigment (Zhang et al., 2019) found in fruits, flowers and vegetables responsible for imparting purple, red and blue colors to the same. This anthocyanin pigment contains phenolic components such as delphinidin, cyanidin, etc., which will undergo structural changes and hence colour changes with pH variations. Compared to its synthetic counterparts like bromophenol blue, the use of anthocyanin extracts as pH indicators offer extra advantages of being nontoxic and ecofriendly in nature (Etxabide, Kilmartin, & Maté, 2021). There are several reports on the use of anthocyanin pigments from different sources such as red cabbage (Liang, Sun, Cao, Li, & Wang, 2019), red raspberry (Sani, Tavassoli, Hamishehkar, & McClements, 2021), purple sweet potato (Choi, Lee, Lacroix, & Han, 2017), mulberry (Zeng et al., 2019), etc. incorporated as pH indicators in biodegradable matrices to make it pH sensitive. In this work, anthocyanin extracted from clitoria ternatea flower (CTE), a plant widely found in nature is used and there are not many reports on the same as pH indicator in biodegradable films.

However, anthocyanin incorporated starch film lack good mechanical properties and suffers from high hydrophilicity. Reinforcing starch matrix with nanofillers is one commonly used technique to overcome these drawbacks. Carbon dots (CD), a promising photoactive nanocarbon materials (1–20 nm) with tunable photoluminescent and electronic properties (Yang et al., 2021) is one such nanofiller which has all the potentiality to be used as nano enhancers in biodegradable matrices. It possesses sp2/sp3 carbon cores surrounded by variety of functional groups like oxygen, nitrogen, sulphur etc. and displays excellent characteristics like high specific surface area, high mechanical flexibility, good chemical and mechanical stability along with excellent solubility, low toxicity and biocompatibility (Li & Dong, 2018). Compared to its 2D counterparts, reinforcing starch matrices with zero dimensional CD offer significant exfoliation and good dispersion in polymer matrices leading to multiple enhancement in mechanical, barrier and optical properties of the matrix (Xu, Xie, Li, & Hakkarainen, 2017). Several natural and synthetic materials are utilized for the production of CD. Compared to its synthetic counterparts, the synthesis of CD from natural resources is always a sought after research area and several natural materials like sesame seeds (Roshni & Divya, 2017), ginger (C. L. Li et al., 2014), lemon (Monte-Filho, Andrade, Lima, & Araujo, 2019), coffee grounds (Wang et al., 2016), etc. have been used to synthesis highly fluorescent CD. Soy protein is one such natural material which can potentially acts an excellent source of nitrogen doped CD due to the presence of abundant amine functional groups. Soy protein is the protein isolated from soy bean and is obtained as a byproduct of soy bean oil production (Xiao, Liu, Kang, Wang, & Xu, 2020). Soy protein is commercially available in three forms based on the protein content; soy flour (SF), soy concentrate (SC) and soy protein isolate (SPI). Among these, SPI has the highest protein content of 90%. Though work on CD from soy flour (Fang et al., 2016) and soy milk (Zhu, Zhai, & Dong, 2012) have been reported there are not many works on CD produced from SPI.

Hence in this work, pH sensitive biodegradable starch films containing CD from SPI and anthocyanin from CTE have been developed. The physico-chemical characterization of CD and CD and/or CTE incorporated colorimetric films were done using modern analysis techniques like UV, PL, XRD, SEM, FTIR etc. The effect of CTE and CD on pH mediated colour variations and detection of ammonia was studied. Finally, the indicator films were used to monitor the freshness of pork stored at 25 °C temperature.

Potato starch ((C6H10O5)n, MW = 162.15) was purchased from Nice Chemicals Private Limited, Ernakulam. Clitoria ternatea flower was plucked from neighbourhood of Alappuzha District. SPI with protein content of 95% was purchased from Adeesh Agrofood, Haryana. 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich Chemical Co. USA. Anthocyanin was extracted from clitoria ternatea flower according to the method suggested by Pourjavaher (Pourjavaher, Almasi, Meshkini, Pirsa, & Parandi, 2017). All other chemicals used were of analytical grade.

CD derived from SPI was prepared using the method adopted by Javanbakth (Javanbakht & Namazi, 2017) with some modifications. 2.5 g of SPI was mixed with 20 mL water and heated in a Teflon equipped hydrothermal autoclave at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling the autoclave, the resulting brown solution was centrifuged at a high speed (10,000 rpm) for 15 min followed by the filtration of upper brown solution using a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The solution was then dried and diluted with 50 mL water and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of starch based indicator films
ce:section.ce:para: Solution casting method was followed for the preparation of starch composite films (Luchese, Sperotto, Spada, & Tessaro, 2017). 6% (w/v) of starch was magnetically stirred in water and 1.9 mL (40% w/v) glycerol was added as the plasticizer. Two concentrations of CD, 9% (w/v) and 17% (w/v) of CD solutions (based on the results obtained by Javanbakth et al. (Javanbakht & Namazi, 2017)) was separately added to the above solution and homogenized (Ika Ultra-Turrax). To each of these solutions 5% (w/v) CTE was added and homogenized again. After that the solution was magnetically stirred for 30 min at 85 °C. Finally, film forming solution was cast onto Teflon plate and dried in an air oven at 55 °C. The film was coded SED1 and SED2.
SE film was prepared using the same procedure without adding CD. Control starch film was prepared without adding CTE and CD.

2.4.1 The absorbance of CD was measured using UV–Visible double beam spectrometer model V-530 (Jasco, Japan) in the scan range 190–900 nm. Fluorescence spectra of CD was analysed using a Shimadzu PL spectrophotometer operated at 280–450 nm range of excitation wavelengths.

The colour parameters of the film sample were determined by SC-80C colorimeter and its L (lightness), a (redness to greenness), b (yellowness to blueness) values were determined. L can have values from 0 (black) to 100 (pure white), a can have value from negative (green) to positive (red) and b can assume value from negative (blue) to positive (yellow) (Luchese et al., 2017) and the total colour difference was calculated using the formula
(1)
Δ E ⋅ = ⋅
(
L 0 ∗
−
L ∗
)
2
+
(
a 0 ∗
−
a ∗
)
2
+
(
b 0 ∗
−
b ∗
)
2
where
L 0 ∗
,
a
0
∗
and
b 0 ∗
were the color parameters of control S film, and
L ∗
,
a ∗
and
b ∗
were the colour parameters of composite films [1].

SEM of composite films surfaces was done using Carl Zeiss Secondary Electron Microscope at an accelerating voltage of 20 kV. Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) of the samples was obtained using Perkin Elmer model 1000 FTIR spectrometer with Universal ATR accessory. IR spectra was collected from the samples within the range of 4000 –500 cm−1. XRD of starch composite films was done using Siemens X-ray diffractometer D5000 instrument using Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation operating at 40 kV and 30 mA. Scattered radiation was detected in the range 2θ = 5°–70° at a scanning rate of 4°/min. Thermal stability of the film samples was determined using thermogravimetric analyser (Shimadzu TGA-Q500 instrument). Approximately 4–6 mg of the sample was heated at 10 °C/min under nitrogen atmosphere (50 mL/min) at 20–900 °C intervals. Tensile properties such as tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break % (E) of each film were measured according to the standard method of ASTM D 882-88 using Instron Universal Testing Machine (Model 5565, Instron Engineering Corporation, Canton, MA, USA). Film sample were cut into rectangular strips (2.54 cm × 15 cm) and the testing machine was operated in tensile mode with an initial grip separation of 50 mm and crosshead speed of 50 mm/min.

Thickness of the film was determined using a micrometer (Dial thickness gauge 7301, Mitutoyo, Japan) at an accuracy of 0.01 mm. Three random measurements were taken for each film and the average values were taken. Moisture content of composite films was measured using a digital moisture analyser (Wensar, PGB 1 MB). Triplicate measurement were done.

Water solubility and degree of swelling were determined following the method of Jamroz et al. (Jamróz, Kulawik, Krzyściak, Talaga-Ćwiertnia, & Juszczak, 2019) with slight modifications. Film samples (5 × 5 cm) was dried in an air oven for 24 h till constant weight was attained (W1). Afterwards the film samples were immersed in 50 mL distilled water for 24 h. After this time period the remaining film samples were picked out, dried with filter paper and weighed (W2). To get final dry weight (W3), the samples were dried to constant weight in an air oven at 70 °C for 24 h and weighed. Triplicate measurement was done. Degree of swelling and film solubility were calculated as
(2)
Degree ⋅ of ⋅ swelling ⋅ = ⋅
W 2 − W 1
W 1
(3)
Solubility = ⋅
W 1 − W 3
W 1
x
100

Antioxidant activity of the composite films were studied using DPPH assay. In this method the ability of the components in the film to bleach the DPPH radical was measured. For this around 10 mg of each films were immersed in 4 mL of 100 μM DPPH methanol solution. After reacting in dark, the absorbance of the reaction solution was recorded at 517 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-sensing ability of CTE solution and SE, SED1, SED2 films
ce:section.ce:para: CTE (1 mL) was dissolved in 30 mL of buffers with different pH values (pH 1.0–12.0) and the photographs of the solutions were taken. The pH-sensing ability of film sample was determined by the method of Luchese et al. (Luchese et al., 2017). For this, film sample was immersed in different buffer solutions (pH 1.0–12.0) for 10 min. Colour parameters L*, a*, b* were recorded using SC-80C colorimeter.

The ability of the indicators to sense ammonia vapors was tested according to the procedure adopted by Ezati et al. (Ezati, Tajik, Moradi, & Molaei, 2019). The indicators were placed 1 cm above 80 mL of aqueous 0.8 M and 1.4 M ammonia solution for 24 min. Colour parameter (R, G, B values) were recorded every 4 min using a Nix Pro 2 colour sensor and the sensitivity percentage (SRGB) of indicator films towards volatile ammonia was calculated as
(4)
SRGB ⋅ = ⋅
(
R 1 − R 2
)
+
(
G 1 − G 2
)
+
( B 1 − B 2 )
R 1 + G 1 + B 1
x ⋅ 100 %
where
R 1
= red,
G 1
= green and B1 = blue were the initial colour parameters of the indicators and
R 2
,
G 2
,
B 2
were the colour parameters after exposing to ammonia.

The colorimetric films were applied to monitor the freshness of pork during storage. Briefly, 30 g fresh pork was placed in a packaging box attached with designed colour label. A digital pH meter was used to monitor the pH variations in the pork sample. Spoilage analysis was conducted by placing the indicator film in the middle space of the colour label and monitoring the colour changes. Thus, there was no direct contact with the pork samples. Then, the packed box was stored in an incubator at 25 °C. Three boxes were prepared for every sampling and the experiment was carried out in triplicate. Colour parameters (L, a, b values) were noted and colour changes of the indicator label was photographed.
The Duncan test and one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) were used for multiple comparisons by SPSS 13.0 software package. Difference was considered as statistically significant if p < 0.05.

Owing to the presence of rich nitrogen containing groups in SPI, SPI was used as a precursor for the synthesis of nitrogen doped CD under hydrothermal condition. This is the first report on the synthesis of CD form SPI. The UV–Visible absorption spectrum of the synthesized CD (Fig. S1(A) in supporting information) shows a strong absorption at 206 nm due to π-π* transition of aromatic C = C bonds. A small hump at 260 nm is attributed to n-π* transition of CO bonds (G. Chen et al., 2016). The solution emitted bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV excitation (inset image in supporting information S1(A)).

The fluorescence spectra of the prepared CD is shown in Fig. S1(B) in supporting information. The prepared CD had high fluorescence and exhibited excitation dependent emission PL spectra with maximum emission at 340 nm excitation. The emission peak is found to shift from 410 nm to 550 nm on changing the excitation wavelength from 280 nm to 500 nm. This excitation dependent emission phenomena can be attributed to the size distribution of CD and/or the presence of different emissive site in CD (G. Chen et al., 2016). The presence of sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbons in the carbon core of CD along with the surface defect caused by the presence of nitrogen and oxygen rich functional groups on the surface of CD can lead to new emission sites leading to red shift of emission wavelength (Yan et al., 2019). The quantum yield of as prepared CD was 10% under excitation wavelength 340 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anthocyanin concentration and colour of CTE in pH buffers
ce:section.ce:para: The anthocyanin content of 20.66 mg cyanidin-3-glucoside equivalent/L extract and an extraction yield of 16.6% based on dry weight of flower was obtained in this study. Fig. 1 (A) shows the colour variations of CTE in different pH buffers (1–12). As evident from the figure, the anthocyanin present in the extract solution exhibited wide colour variations ranging from red in pH (1–3), purple in pH (4–5), blue in pH (6–7), green in pH (8–9), colourless in pH (10–11) and yellow in pH (11–12). This colour changes is due to structural changes undergone by anthocyanin in different pH medium where it exhibits a red flavilium cationic structure at pH (1–3), a purple/blue quinoidal anhydrobase base at pH (4–7), green chalcone at pH (8–9), carbinol base at pH (10–11) and yellow chalcone at pH (12) (Kurek et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical appearance and color of the films
ce:section.ce:para: The physical appearance of the films is an important parameter which determines the effectiveness of the film in becoming a good candidate for packaging applications. Fig. 1(B) shows the physical appearance of S, SE, SED1 and SED2 films. Starch film incorporated with CTE and CD were visually transparent and homogenous with no visible cracks or bubbles and could easily be peeled off from the Teflon plate. Visually the composite films were transparent, though they turned red and purple on incorporating CTE and CD. The control film had L* value 70.20 ± 0.52, a* value 4.18 ± 0.45 and b* value −2.54 ± 0.53. SE and SED1/SED2 films had lower L* value ranging from 57.62 to 52.64 and b* value in the range −9.53 to −12.77. As compared with control starch film, SE and both SED films had lower L* and b* values proving the transition of colourless starch film to pink and purple colour. On the other hand, ΔE value of films containing CTE was higher than 5 indicating that the film has become darker in colour and the colour change is visible by naked eye (Luchese, Abdalla, Spada, & Tessaro, 2018). The a* value of SE film was the highest among the films confirming its red colour (Jamróz et al., 2019). Colour parameter values were in good agreement with the physical appearances of the films.

The influence of CTE and CD on surface morphology of starch films was studied by SEM (Fig. 2 ). The control starch film has smooth and compact surface due to homogenous dispersion of glycerol in the matrix. With the addition of the extract, the surface became rougher. CTE anthocyanin may break the bonds between starch molecules and increase its brittleness. Clear incorporation of spherical CD could be seen in the SEM photo of SED1 and SED2 films. However, compared to SED1 film, agglomeration can be observed in SED2 film. The higher concentration of CD affects the ordered arrangement of starch chains destroying the flexible and uniform surface.

Fig. 3 (A) shows the FTIR spectrum of control starch and starch composite films. The control S film has characteristic peaks at 3256 cm−1 (O–H stretching), 2919 cm−1 (C–H stretching from alkyl group), 1634 cm−1 (bound water), 1150 cm−1, 1000 cm−1, 996 cm−1 (C–O stretching). There is not much difference in the FTIR spectrum of synthesized SE/SED1/SED2 composite films and the control S film, only slight changes in the intensity and peak position can be observed in composite films. The broad absorption band in the range 3200–3500 cm−1is attributed to inter and intramolecular hydrogen bonded OH groups in plasticizer starch blends and the intensity of this absorption band is found to be slightly decreased in starch composites indicating the formation of hydrogen bonding between OH groups present in starch and oxygen containing groups in extract and CD. The peak at 1634 cm−1 in control starch film attributed to OH bending of incorporated water molecules is found to be slightly shifted to 1650/1647/1640 cm−1 in composite film showing the hydrogen bonding interaction between water molecules and extract/CD. However, no new peaks were observed on adding both CTE and CD in starch matrix revealing that no new covalent bonds were formed between starch and CTE/CD. Wu et al. (Wu et al., 2019) also reported that only electrostatic and hydrogen bonding interactions occurred without any covalent bond formation when chitosan/oxidized chitin nanocrystals and black rice bran anthocyanins were added into chitosan matrix. Several other authors have cited good interaction a reason for similar spectrums shown by starch and starch composites (Ezati et al., 2019; Luchese et al., 2018; Silva-Pereira, Teixeira, Pereira-Júnior, & Stefani, 2015).

Fig. 3(B) shows the XRD spectra of pure starch film and starch composites. The characteristic peaks of control starch film appear at 16.71°, 19.69° and 21.92°. The nature of the peaks confirms the amorphous structure of starch. When CTE and CD are added to starch matrix, there is no change in the amorphous peak of starch showing homogenous distribution of CTE and CD in the matrix. From the literature (J. Chen et al., 2019), the characteristic peak of CD occurs at 2θ = 26.69° attributed to the ordered crystalline structure of CD. The absence of this peak of CD in the composite film may be due to the exfoliation and homogenous dispersion of CD in the matrix. Also the concentration of CD in the matrix is very low to cause crystallinity and agglomeration.

TGA curves of starch and starch composite films are shown in Fig. 4 . All the films showed three stages of weight loss. The weight loss in the first stage (<100 °C) is attributed to the loss of free water. The second stage weight loss occurs between 200 and 300 °C and is assigned to the evaporation of glycerol and bound water. Finally, the final weight loss between 350 and 400 °C is attributed to the depolymerisation, decomposition and breaking of functional groups associated with the polymer chain. Jha et al. (Jha, 2020) has also observed similar degradation pattern in starch-chitosan-nanoclay bionanocomposite film. Addition of CD was found to enhance the thermal stability of starch film, the residual mass in SED1 was 10.7% while the residual mass in control starch was 6.5%. This increase in thermal stability can be attributed to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonding between functional groups in CD and the matrix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical properties of starch and starch indicator films
ce:section.ce:para: The physical properties of the films are shown in Table 1 . All the films showed uniform thickness indicating homogenous distribution of fillers in the matrix. Addition of CTE was found to increase the hydrophilic properties of the film than the control starch film, while the addition of CD was found to decrease the same. SED1 and SED2 films showed significantly lower (p < 0.05) moisture content and solubility than control film. This suggests the formation of crosslinking and network formation between starch and CD molecules. The hydroxyl group of starch interacts with the hydroxyl groups on CD reducing the availability of hydroxyl groups to interact with water (Zhang et al., 2019). In the case of swelling it is found to increase with the addition of CTE which according to Halasz et al. (Halász & Csóka, 2018) is beneficial as aqueous media which changed pH can penetrate into the bulk matrix and increase the pH sensitivity.

Fig. 5 (A) shows the tensile strength and elongation at break values of starch and starch indicator films. As evident from the figure, both the tensile strength and elongation at break of starch film increases on addition of CTE and/CD. The addition of CD was found to enhance the tensile strength significantly. Chen et al. (J. Chen et al., 2019) and Javanbakth et al. (Javanbakht & Namazi, 2017) have shown that tensile strength of starch film increases on adding graphene quantum dots. The tensile strength was found to increase on adding 9% CD after which it decreases slightly. At 9% CD, homogenous distribution of CD in the starch matrix takes place and the resulting SED film has sufficient strength to absorb the given force. But at higher concentration (17%) agglomeration takes place as evident from SEM figures. Starch film containing CTE was found to have highest elongation at break value among the four films. This might be attributed to the plasticizing effect of the extract which can reduce the intermolecular forces between starch molecules increasing the mobility of starch molecules.

Fig. 5(B) shows the DPPH radical scavenging ability of starch and starch composite films. As evident from the figure, the DPPH radical scavenging ability of starch matrix increased significantly with the addition of CTE and/CD showing an enhancement in the antioxidant property of the films with the addition of anthocyanin and nanofillers. In alcoholic stimulant, the DPPH radical scavenging activity of starch films increased from 7% to 72% with the addition of both CTE and CD. There was no significant difference on the activity between CTE and CD. This shows that the antioxidant activity arises mainly due to the presence of anthocyanin in the matrix. Anthocyanin contains ample phenolic hydroxyl groups containing large number of electrons and anthocyanin can donate these electron and hydrogen atoms to reactive free radicals and hence quench the free radicals (Yun et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colour responses of SE, SED1, SED2 films to pH changes
ce:section.ce:para: As stated earlier, anthocyanin undergoes structural changes in different pH solutions and hence films incorporated with anthocyanin is bound to undergo such similar changes in pH buffer solutions. Fig. 6 (A) shows the colour changes undergone by CTE anthocyanin immobilized starch film (SE, SED1 and SED2) in different pH buffer solutions. All the three films exhibited similar visible colour changes ranging from red to yellow on changing pH from 1 to 12. The films showed reddish pink at pH 1–3, purple at pH 4–7, bluish green at pH 8–11 and yellow at pH 12. The colour parameter values are plotted in Fig. 6(B). As evident from the figure, all the films with pH sensitive anthocyanin exhibited a high L* value indicating high brightness. On the contrary, the a* value was high at low pH and decreased with increase in pH indicating that at low pH the films exhibited red colour. In fact, the a* values start decreasing at pH 5 (5.73 ± 0.38) and the decrease become more evident at pH 12 (−16.07 ± 0.95) turning to green. Similar trend in a* value was given by Kurek et al., 2018, who noticed a bluish green colour in the pH range 8–10 for starch/chitosan/anthocyanin film. The films also exhibited higher negative b* values from pH 3 to pH 11 indicating a bluish intensity. The ΔE values of all three films at pH range 1–12 (Table 2 in supporting information) were greater than 5 confirming the fact that the colour changes undergone by the indicator films were detectable by human eye and the occurrence of ΔE value above 12 for most pH range shows that the absolute colour changes were noticeable even by untrained panellists (Ezati & Rhim, 2020). These results shows than CTE could be used as intelligent pH indicator for monitoring freshness of food.

In order to mimic the response of fabricated indicator films towards volatile nitrogen compounds produced during spoilage of pork samples, the sensitivity and speed of the films to different concentrations of ammonia was measured. Fig. 7 shows the sensitivity of anthocyanin containing starch films towards ammonia. At 0.8 M and 1.4 M ammonia concentrations, the SRGB value increased significantly during the first 4 min indicating rapid response and high sensitivity to ammonia vapors. Ammonia solution diffuses into the film matrix, gets hydrated to form hydrated ammonia (NH3.H2O) which then hydrolyse to produce ammonium ions (NH4 +) and hydroxyl ions (OH−) according to the equation
NH3+H2O→NH4 ++OH−

This NH4 + induces an alkaline environment on the film surface and the OH− forms bond with the phenolic OH of anthocyanin to form phenolic oxygen anion (Ezati & Rhim, 2020). The SRGB score of all the three starch based indicators at 0.8 M and 1.4 M was at least 400% higher than the SRGB score of roscelle anthocyanin immobilized starch polyvinyl alcohol film (Zhang et al., 2019). At 4 min, for 0.8 M ammonia concentration, the score reached the maximum value 78.32 ± 1.52, 87.52 ± 1.13, 88.96 ± 2.45 for SE, SED1 and SED2 films respectively while for 1.4 M concentration it was 80.91 ± 1.20, 90.52 ± 0.45 and 89.56 ± 0.25 respectively. The rapid and high sensitivity of the present indicator films suggest its applicability to be used as freshness monitor in intelligent packaging.

The performance of SE, SED1 and SED2 indicators for monitoring pork freshness was studied by fitting it into transparent packages (Fig. 8 ). The colour the indicators changed from pink/purple (fresh stage) for SE and SED1/SED2 respectively to green colour on day 2 (spoiled stage). According to previous reports (Choi et al., 2017) (Yun et al., 2019) it is found that the spoilage of meat product occurs due to microbial action. This microbial contamination can lead to the production of high level of total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) which in turn changes the pH level of pork. Hence our intelligent pH sensitive films SE, SED1 and SED2 can be used to monitor the freshness of pork. The pH of the sample maintained at a temperature of 25 °C was monitored using pH meter. The fresh pork is found to have an acidic pH of 5.8. After 30 h of storage the pork starts deteriorating and the pH shifts to 7. Complete spoilage of pork occurs at 48 h of storage and the pH at this stage is 8. Ma et al. (Ma, Du, & Wang, 2017) have shown that TVB-N value of pork increases to 27.39 mg/100 g at 48 h and at this stage protein in pork start to decompose to produce organic amines. Microbial degradation results in the production of ammonia, trimethyl, dimethyl and other volatile amines due to decomposition of lipid and proteins thus increasing the TVB-N. Thus, the pork is fresh between pH 5–6, and spoilage occurs from pH 7. This is consistent from previous reports where Liu et al. (Liu et al., 2018) and Yun et al. (Yun et al., 2019) have reported an increase in pH value from 5.8 to 7.6 as storage time of chicken and pork increases. Owing to the ability of our pH indicator films to change its colour with pH changes, the fabricated films (SE, SED1, SED2) were used to monitor the freshness of stored pork.

In this study, food grade multifunctional packaging films were developed by incorporating soy protein isolate derived CD and anthocyanin from CTE in starch matrix. This is the first report on the synthesis of CD from soy protein isolate and the incorporation of the same in a pH indicator film. CD derived from soy protein isolate had good fluorescence and exhibited excitation dependent emission. The physical properties of starch film were greatly enhanced by the addition of both CD and CTE. FTIR and SEM analysis showed that both CD and CTE were well compatible with the matrix. Incorporation of CTE in the matrix made the film pH sensitive but also increased the its water sensitivity. Addition of CD was found to decrease the water sensitivity and increase the mechanical strength of the film. SE and SED films were found to be antioxidants. The intelligent indicator film made by the combination of starch, CD and CTE could be used to monitor the freshness of packed pork sample. Due to environment friendly nature of this indicator film, this smart packaging is of interest to consumers, retailers and manufacturers. The food products can be disposed when they are not suitable for consuming just by visual monitoring of the indicator film and this reduces wastage of food products by accurate monitoring of best before date. The future studies should focus on the stability of anthocyanin in the matrix and should explore additional potential applications of indicator films. The advanced development of this indicator film through integration with commercial screen printing and packaging should also be the future focus in the field of intelligent packaging.

On behalf of all authors, I declare that the individual contributions of all authors of manuscript entitled “Preparation of pH sensitive film based on starch/carbon nano dots incorporating anthocyanin for monitoring spoilage of pork” are as below:
Rekha Rose Koshy= Concept, Methodology, and writing of original draft
Jijo Thomas Koshy= Software and figures monitoring
Siji K Mary = Review and color analysis
Dr. Sandeep Sadanandhan= Manuscript modification
Dr. Jisha S. = Antioxidant analysis
Dr. Laly A. Pothen=Project administration

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors have not received any funding for doing this work.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodcont.2021.108039.
